Tafseer:
1. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

'Praise be to Allah, to Whom belongs all that is in the heavens and all that is in the earth, and to Him be praise in the Hereafter. And He is the Wise, the All-Aware.' (1)

We have already pointed out that the foundation of this Surah is built upon gratitude (shukr) and its requirements and implications. (In the commentary of Surah Al-Fatihah, it has also been clarified that Tawhid, and indeed the entire structure of religion, is established upon gratitude. The obligation of thanking the benefactor is a self-evident truth of human nature. Whenever someone receives a favor, they are naturally grateful and indebted. If someone is not grateful to their benefactor, they are deemed mean and ungrateful. Based on this principle, the question arises: Who created all these blessings of the heavens and the earth, which human beings benefit from at every moment and upon which their very survival depends, and in whose power do they lie? Who made this sun, this moon, these clouds, this wind, these stars, and these planets? Where did this earth, these rivers, these mountains, these beasts and birds, these trees and streams, and these greens and flowers come from? Obviously, the correct answer to these questions is that all these things are created by Allah Almighty, are owned by Him, and are under His control. In the previous Surahs, you have read that even the most stubborn opponents of the Qur'an gave this very same answer to these questions. In this verse, the Qur'an reminds us of this self-evident truth that the same Allah Who is the Creator and Owner of everything in the heavens and the earth is also the true deserving recipient of the gratitude of all creations that are benefiting from these things created by Him. The essential requirement of this gratitude is that no one else has any share in the creation or management of these things. If human beings receive any benefit from others, they receive it merely as a medium and source. For this reason, if any right of theirs is established upon humans, it is subordinate to the right of God, not superior to or equal to it.

'and to Him be praise in the Hereafter': The preceding clause mentioned the right of Allah that is established upon every servant in this worldly life. Now, this clause mentions His right that will be manifest in the Hereafter. It states that His praise will also occur in the Hereafter. Several points are clarified by this clause:

First, the elaborate arrangement of Lordship (Rububiyyah) that Allah has made in this world logically demands that the Hereafter must manifest after it. If there were no Hereafter, this entire Lordship would remain completely meaningless and without purpose. This point has been explained in several places, so an allusion is sufficient here.

Second, this refers to the chorus of praise from the believers that will rise from their tongues in the Hereafter after the manifestation of all realities and the fulfillment of all of Allah's promises. This is pointed out in Surah Yunus: 'And the close of their call will be: Praise be to Allah, Lord of the Worlds' (Yunus: 10).

Third, this is a complete negation of partners and intercessors—that all these assumed deities, upon whose hope of intercession the polytheists sit complacently, will vanish in the Hereafter. None of them will be of any use to anyone. On that Day, the polytheists will curse their deities, and the deities will declare their disassociation from their worshippers. Everyone will be presented before the One Allah. His decision will be final, and the reality will become manifest to all that Allah alone, the Lord of the Worlds, is worthy of praise. This reality is pointed out in Surah Al-Qasas: 'To Him belongs all praise in the first [life] and the Hereafter, and His is the decision, and to Him you will be returned' (Al-Qasas: 70).

'And He is the Wise, the All-Aware': This presents proof for all the above claims from the attributes of Allah Almighty—that He is Wise (Hakeem) and All-Aware (Khabeer). Therefore, it is necessary for Him to bring such a Day in which He rewards His grateful servants for their gratitude, and the ungrateful face the punishment for their ungratefulness. If this were not so, this world would be reduced to a mere playground or a highly unjust game, and—God forbid—we would have to assume that its Creator is not wise but a mere player, whereas every atom of this universe testifies that its Creator is Wise. Similarly, His being Wise demands that no one's false intercession can nullify His absolute justice, because even in that case, His being Wise would be negated. Furthermore, He is also All-Aware, because when He has created everything, it is necessary that He is aware of its every atom, every movement, and every state of rest. As He says: 'Does He who created not know?' (Al-Mulk: 14). The necessary requirement of His all-encompassing knowledge is that He does not need anyone else's knowledge or report when deciding anyone's matter, nor can anyone deceive Him with their false intercession.

It should also be kept in mind that all these matters are stated in a restrictive style, which means that when the true Creator, Owner, and the true Wise and All-Aware is He alone, then how can anyone else be deserving of praise beside Him?

'He knows what penetrates into the earth and what emerges from it and what descends from the heaven and what ascends into it. And He is the Merciful, the Forgiving.' (2)

This is an explanation of the attribute of being All-Aware (Khabeer)—that His knowledge encompasses every atom and every movement and state of rest in this universe. He is aware of the seed that is placed in the earth, and He knows the plant that emerges from it. Similarly, the good and evil that descend from heaven are also in His knowledge, and He is aware of the things that ascend into it. The meaning is that His knowledge encompasses all universals and particulars, and He is supervising everything. In His kingdom, not even the smallest thing is outside the sphere of His knowledge, nor is it possible for any kind of movement or interference to occur in any corner of it without His knowledge and permission. This encompassing nature of divine knowledge has been explained for the purpose of striking down one of the greatest factors of shirk (polytheism), which is the misconception of the polytheists: how can God be aware at every moment of every corner of such an endless universe, every word and deed, and everyone's pain and suffering! Because of this, according to their own imagination, they divided different parts of this universe among different deities. To seek His proximity and to inform Him of their needs, they invented means and intermediaries. Believing that the Jinn bring news from the heavens, they worshipped them, and considering the angels to be intercessors, they gave them the status of goddesses. This verse strikes down all these superstitions by stating that God's knowledge encompasses everything, and therefore, He has no partner or associate. He Himself encompasses the entire system of His universe and is alone sufficient.

'And He is the Merciful, the Forgiving': That is, God is neither unjust nor unfair such that the effort and intercession of someone else would be required to please Him or to save oneself from His calamities; rather, He is extremely Merciful and Forgiving. To attract His mercy and attain His forgiveness, it is sufficient for the servant to ask Him for forgiveness for their sins, and to turn to Him and reform themselves. Here, the reality should be kept in view that another major factor of polytheism is the misconception of the polytheists who imagined God as an extremely terrifying being, and then, to keep Him pleased, they invented means and methods according to their own imaginations. This clause strikes at this very illusion.

'But those who disbelieve say, "The Hour will not come to us." Say, "Yes, by my Lord, it will surely come to you. [Allah is] the Knower of the unseen." Not absent from Him is an atom's weight within the heavens or within the earth or [what is] smaller than that or greater, except that it is in a clear register.' (3)

That is, when Allah is Wise and All-Aware, and His knowledge encompasses everything, the logical requirement of this is that the Resurrection must surely come, but those who are stubborn are not ready to accept it and deny it with great arrogance, saying that the Resurrection will never come.

'Say, "Yes, by my Lord, it will surely come to you"': With the same force and emphasis with which the deniers made their denial, the Prophet (pbuh) is commanded to reply with an oath, telling them: 'Yes, by my Lord! It will surely come upon you.'

'the Knower of the unseen...': 'The Knower of the unseen' serves as an apposition (badal) for 'my Lord'—meaning, by my Lord who is aware of all that is unseen, from whom not even an atom's weight of anything in the heavens or the earth is hidden, but rather everything is recorded in a highly clear register.

In this verse, the description of the vastness of divine knowledge is meant to warn the deniers so that they become alert, remembering that they are denying the Resurrection with such audacity, and to remind them that not only will it come, but everyone will also have to give an account of their every word and deed, whether small or great. At the same time, it corrects a misconception: the deniers found the Resurrection highly improbable because they wondered who could possibly have knowledge of every word and deed of every single person in such a vast world to sit down and reckon everyone! To remove this misconception, reference is made here to Allah's all-encompassing knowledge, just as it was done above in the context of Tawhid.

'That He may reward those who believe and do righteous deeds. Those will have forgiveness and noble provision. But those who strive against Our verses [seeking] to cause failure - those will have a punishment of painful filth.' (4-5)

This explains the necessity of the Resurrection—why its coming is essential. It states that it is necessary because if it does not come, it would mean that in the sight of God, the righteous and the wicked are both equal, whereas this is self-evidently false. This world is not a place of lawlessness; hence, it is essential that a Day comes in which Allah Almighty rewards those of faith and righteous deeds for their goodness, and punishes those who tried to frustrate His words for their failed efforts.

Here, it is noteworthy that the primary purpose of the Resurrection is stated as rewarding the believers. This is because this is indeed its primary objective. Punishing the criminals is not its primary goal, but rather one of its necessary consequences. Allah Almighty created this world for mercy, and for this mercy He has also set the Day of the Hereafter, but the logical consequence of this is that those who do not make themselves deserving of this mercy will deserve His punishment.

Two things are mentioned here for the believers: first, forgiveness; second, noble provision. 'Forgiveness' means that Allah Almighty will overlook the lapses and mistakes they committed while leading a life of faith and righteous deeds. 'Noble provision' is a comprehensive expression for all the bounties and favors they will inherit in Paradise.

The disbelievers are mentioned here with the attribute: 'those who strive against Our verses [seeking] to cause failure'. 'Mu'ajazah' means competing with one another with the intention of defeating the other. The meaning is that those whose day and night efforts were dedicated to defeating the verses and words of Allah, He will give them a share of the punishment of 'painful filth' (rajz aleem). Obviously, this refers to the leaders of disbelief for whom there will be a specific punishment. This specific punishment is expressed as 'a punishment of painful filth.' 'Rajz' refers to a punishment that is extremely terrifying.

'And those who have been given knowledge see that what is revealed to you from your Lord is the truth, and it guides to the path of the Exalted, the Praiseworthy.' (6)

This mentions those who were blessed with the light of true knowledge, whether they were from the People of the Book who preserved the knowledge of their prophets and scriptures, or from those of sound nature whose hearts were enlightened by the light of the Qur'an due to their uprightness. It states that these people consider what has been sent down to you as the absolute truth. That is, they support the Tawhid to which you invite and the Resurrection of which you warn, testifying that this is the truth, and that those who, relying on their assumed partners and intercessors, deny Tawhid and Resurrection with great arrogance, are entirely upon falsehood. This is said here for the consolation of the Prophet (pbuh)—that if careless and indifferent people oppose you, do not worry about it; it is sufficient for your satisfaction that those who possess the light of knowledge and wisdom are your supporters. A person should care about the wise, not the foolish and useless.

'and it guides to the path of the Exalted, the Praiseworthy': That is, these people of knowledge openly accept the reality that this Book undoubtedly guides to the path of the Exalted, Praiseworthy God. In other words, the religions that others have fabricated lead entirely to the pit of deviation and destruction, whereas this Book shows the way to God. Two attributes of Allah Almighty are mentioned here: first, 'the Exalted' (Al-Aziz), and second, 'the Praiseworthy' (Al-Hameed). 'Al-Aziz' expresses His power and might, and 'Al-Hameed' expresses His being worthy of praise in both this world and the Hereafter, and both of these attributes logically imply Tawhid and the Resurrection, as is clear from the above discussions.

This verse also clarifies the reality that true knowledge, in the terminology of the Qur'an, is only the gnosis of God and the knowledge of the Hereafter. If someone does not possess this knowledge, then no matter how many other sciences they study, all of this knowledge of theirs is a burden for themselves and a danger for others. For this reason, the Qur'an calls 'scholars' (ulama) only those who possess the gnosis of God and the Hereafter:

'Only those fear Allah, from among His servants, who have knowledge. Indeed, Allah is Exalted in Might and Forgiving.' (Fatir: 28)

'But those who disbelieve say, "Shall we direct you to a man who will inform you that when you have been disintegrated in complete disintegration, you will indeed be [recreated] in a new creation?"' (7)

After pointing out the way of the believers, this now describes the way of those who are deprived of the light of knowledge and are wandering in the darkness of disbelief. It states that their condition is such that they mock you and your invitation. They say to the people: 'Come, let us show you a crazy man who, acting as a messenger of God, goes about proclaiming that when people die and become completely disintegrated, they will be raised alive anew!' It is as if, in their view, this statement is not even worthy of being refuted with any argument.

'Has he invented about Allah a lie or is there in him a madness?" Rather, they who do not believe in the Hereafter are in punishment and extreme error.' (8)

This is a further detail of their mockery and its fitting reply from Allah Almighty.

'Has he invented about Allah a lie or is there in him a madness?': That is, they say that this man's matter is not free from two states: either it must be accepted that he has fabricated a lie against God by claiming that God has sent him as a messenger and that whatever he says is on His behalf, or it must be accepted that this is a type of madness in which this man is afflicted! The meaning is that they imply both of these things are present in this man at the same time: he is both a fabricator and a madman.

'Rather, they who do not believe in the Hereafter...': The Qur'an immediately gave a reply to the mockery of these rebels, and see how dignified and effective a reply it is!

It states that these foolish people made a very wrong diagnosis. The fault lies neither in the caller nor in the call; rather, all the fault lies within these very people who do not believe in the Hereafter. They are in punishment and extreme error. That is, one type of error is that from which there remains a possibility of return and reform, even if after a thousand faults. But the one who is afflicted with the punishment of the Hereafter has no possibility of return left. All doors of hope are closed for them forever. This end of theirs is expressed in the present tense style rather than the future tense, because this end of theirs is inherent in their attitude itself. It is as if today, because of their arrogance, they are already facing it.

'Have they not looked at what is before them and what is behind them of the heaven and the earth? If We should will, We could cause the earth to swallow them or let fall upon them fragments from the heaven. Indeed in that is a sign for every servant who turns [to Allah].' (9)

What was stated in the first verse of this group of verses is repeated in a new style at the end of the group. In the first verse, it was stated that whatever is in the heavens and the earth belongs to God alone, and therefore in this world the true deserving recipient of gratitude is He, and in the Hereafter praise will also be His. Here, it is said: Have these arrogant people never reflected on the reality that this heaven which is stretched over their heads like a canopy, and this earth which is spread beneath their feet like a carpet, and from whose benefits and blessings they are benefiting, do not remain in place by their own holding, but rather Allah alone is holding them? If Allah had not held them, both of these would have become a source of destruction for them instead of blessings. It states that if He wills, He can cause the earth to swallow them, and if He wills, instead of raining down the rain of mercy from this heaven, He can rain down stones upon them. Nothing in this universe is benefiting humans automatically, but rather by the command of God, and nothing is directly under human control, but rather by God's command it is active in providing benefit to them. This is an obvious reality which obligates humans not to boast over any blessing, but to remain grateful to their Lord Who bestowed this blessing upon them, and to use it in His pleasure and obedience instead of making it a means of disobedience and rebellion against God.

This same theme is stated in the following Surah—Surah Fatir, which is its counterpart—as follows:

'Indeed, Allah holds the heavens and the earth, lest they cease. And if they should cease, no one could hold them after Him. Indeed, He is Forbearing and Forgiving.' (Fatir: 41)

'Indeed in that is a sign for every servant who turns [to Allah]': That is, if they were to reflect on this aspect of the heavens and the earth, there is a very great proof within it for that to which the Qur'an is inviting them. But to access this proof, it is necessary that a person possesses the quest for truth, the capacity to take warning, and a heart that turns to Him. For those who do not possess these qualities, no sign can be of any use.

2. Subsequent Theme: Verses 10–21

Next, two examples from history are presented. The first example is of Hazrat Dawud and Hazrat Sulaiman (peace be upon them), whom Allah Almighty blessed with a great kingdom, great knowledge and wisdom, and countless blessings of the heavens and the earth, and upon receiving these blessings, they did not fall into rebellion and corruption, but remained constantly grateful and obedient to their Lord. As a reward for this gratitude of theirs, Allah Almighty kept increasing His blessings upon them.

The second example is of the people of Saba. They were also given the government of a highly populated and fertile land, but upon receiving it, they fell into rebellion and corruption, and ultimately, Allah Almighty sent a flood upon them and destroyed them in such a way that they were reduced to a mere tale of the past.

Both of these examples are presented before the wealthy and arrogant of the Quraysh, showing that both of these paths are open before them. If they wish, they can make themselves deserving of God's blessings by remaining grateful to God like Hazrat Dawud (pbuh) and Hazrat Sulaiman (pbuh), and if they wish, they can invite His wrath by adopting the way of the people of Saba. At the same time, this is a reminder that when these examples of the manifestation of God's law of retribution exist in this world, why do you consider its manifestation in the Hereafter to be improbable? Recite the verses in this light.

3. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

'And We certainly gave David from Us bounty. [We said], "O mountains, repeat [Our praises] with him, and the birds [as well]." And We made pliable for him iron.' (10)

The details of the special favor that Allah Almighty bestowed upon Hazrat Dawud (pbuh) and Hazrat Sulaiman (pbuh) have already been mentioned in Surah Al-Anbiya and Surah An-Naml. Here, we will limit our discussion only to the necessary extent.

'"O mountains, repeat [Our praises] with him, and the birds [as well]"': This points to the intense devotion and deep feeling that Allah Almighty had bestowed upon Hazrat Dawud (pbuh)—that when he sat in the lap of the mountains and sang the praises of his Lord in his special divine melody and recited his poetic supplications, the trees, rocks, beasts, and birds would all sway and join in harmony with him.

'Ta'weeb' lexically means 'tarjee'—meaning to join one's voice with someone else's tune, to repeat their voice, and to harmonize with them. Although everything in this universe glorifies God, and when it glorifies Him, it naturally harmonizes with others who glorify Him, yet just as Allah Almighty had bestowed upon Hazrat Dawud a special kind of tender heart and a special type of melody, He had also, by His special command, commanded the mountains and the birds to join him in glorifying and praising his Lord whenever Hazrat Dawud did so. In Surah Al-Anbiya, this same theme is expressed as: 'And We subjected the mountains to exalt [Us], along with David, and the birds' (79).

'And We made pliable for him iron': After describing the state of his assembly of peace, this describes his state of war—that We made iron soft for him. Its explanation has been given in Surah Al-Anbiya—that he advanced the art of melting iron and making coats of mail from extremely fine links to such an extent that coats of mail became so loose and flexible that they appeared to be made of cloth, being highly easy to wear while providing the protection of iron.

"[Commanding him], 'Make full coats of mail and calculate [precisely] the links, and work [all of you] righteousness. Indeed, I am, of what you do, Seeing.'" (11)

'Sabighat' refers to a loose-fitting garment that covers the entire body. Here, it is used as an attribute for loose coats of mail. In 'qaddir fis-sard', 'sard' means construction, and 'taqdeer' means observing complete proportion in construction.

To prepare a garment of iron that is both loose-fitting and has complete proportion observed in its links was impossible unless Allah Almighty had revealed to Hazrat Dawud the advanced science of melting iron, the credit of whose initiation belongs to him. With this invention of his, he made a highly valuable addition to defensive weaponry, due to which his military strength increased greatly against his rivals.

'and work [all of you] righteousness. Indeed, I am, of what you do, Seeing': In the preceding clause, encouragement was given to develop this art as much as possible and benefit from it. In this clause, the moral requirement of this art is stated—that upon receiving it, do not go astray and do not make it a means of corruption in the land, but rather remember constantly that whatever you do, Allah is watching it. This instruction was given by Allah Almighty to Hazrat Dawud and all his family and followers. This is also mentioned repeatedly in the Psalms and Proverbs.

In this era, the progress that man has made in science cannot be denied, nor can it be denied that great benefits have been achieved and can be achieved from it. But man has forgotten two things: first, that every discovery of science that has occurred has occurred through the guidance of Allah Almighty; second, that the self-evident requirement of every blessing and every power is that man considers it as a trust from God and uses it while remembering that the God Who bestowed it is watching where he uses it. Due to forgetting these two realities, science has now become a great threat to man, and it cannot be said when man might commit suicide with the very weapons of his own invention.

'And to Solomon [We subjected] the wind - its morning journey was one month and its evening journey was one month, and We made flow for him a spring of [liquid] copper. And among the jinn were those who worked before him by the permission of his Lord. And whoever of them deviated from Our command - We made him taste of the punishment of the Blaze.' (12)

Just as Allah Almighty taught Hazrat Dawud (pbuh) the highly advanced art of using iron, with which he made an unparalleled addition to his land power, He bestowed upon Hazrat Sulaiman (pbuh) the art of controlling the wind, with which he developed his navy to such an extent that his ships carried out journeys of months without any hindrance. Under the commentary of verse 81 of Surah Al-Anbiya, we have mentioned that his sailing ships traveled to highly distant coasts, and their sails were constructed in such a scientific manner that no hindrance occurred in their journey due to the lack, intensity, or opposition of the wind. In Surah Sad, it is stated: 'So We subjected to him the wind blowing by his command gently, wherever he directed' (36). In Surah Al-Anbiya, it is stated: 'And to Solomon [We subjected] the wind, blowing forcefully, proceeding by his command toward the land which We had blessed' (81).

In the verse under discussion, the length of the journey of these ships is pointed out—that their individual trips extended over a long period of a month. Although the wind is mentioned, the objective is the going and coming of the ships. It is as if by mentioning the primary agent, the object acted upon is pointed out, because Hazrat Sulaiman's (pbuh) primary control was manifest in the winds. Obviously, these long journeys were possible only when these ships were highly large and the system of controlling the wind with them was so advanced and robust that they could successfully face every type of wind in all kinds of seas. It should also be kept in mind here that in eloquent Arabic, the words 'ghaduw' and 'rawah' are also used free from the restriction of morning and evening, carrying the general meaning of going and coming. For example: 'And [remember] when you left your family in the morning to post the believers at their stations for the battle' (Al-Imran: 121). Similarly, regarding the word 'rawah', the author of *Aqrab al-Mawarid* wrote: 'And it is sometimes used to mean general departure and going.'

This detail clarifies that by the subjection of the wind, it is not meant the mere subjection of the wind; rather, just as above, making iron soft for Hazrat Dawud was meant as an allusion to his weapon-making and military power—as is also clarified in another place: 'And We taught him the fashioning of coats of mail to protect you from your [mutual] violence' (Al-Anbiya: 80)—similarly, the subjection of the wind for Hazrat Sulaiman is meant as an allusion to the strength and glory of his navy, and we have already pointed out the state of the vastness of his navy in the commentary of Surah Al-Anbiya.

'and We made flow for him a spring of [liquid] copper': 'Qitr' refers to copper. The role of copper in cultural development needs no explanation. From the study of the history of that era, it is learned that a very large amount of copper was extracted during the time of Hazrat Sulaiman (pbuh), and he used it beautifully in his cultural and construction projects. The verse suggests that this copper was extracted in liquid form and then solidified to be used for various needs. Below, large cauldrons and vessels are mentioned; obviously, they were made of it. In the construction of the Temple (Heikal) as well, as is known from history, Hazrat Sulaiman (pbuh) used this metal extensively. Today, Allah Almighty has caused springs of oil to flow for the Arabs; for Hazrat Sulaiman (pbuh), He had caused a spring of copper to flow. All the treasures of the earth that were discovered in the past, are being discovered today, or will be discovered in the future, are and will be discovered through the guidance bestowed by Allah alone. Those whose eyes are on reality know that the source of everything is Allah Almighty alone, but those whose eyes have been dazed by science think that all this is their own doing.

'And among the jinn were those who worked before him by the permission of his Lord...': It has been mentioned in the commentary of Surah An-Naml that Allah Almighty had also bestowed upon Hazrat Sulaiman (pbuh) such knowledge through which he controlled the rebellious Jinn and used them in his various works. From this verse, it is learned that these Jinn were not directly under the control of Hazrat Sulaiman (pbuh), but rather they obeyed him by the command of Allah Almighty, and if any disobedience was committed by them, Allah Almighty Himself punished them: 'And whoever of them deviated from Our command - We made him taste of the punishment of the Blaze.' Some people have interpreted 'Jinn' to mean giant and powerful human beings, but this view is incorrect. This is because in Surah An-Naml, it is clearly stated that the army of Hazrat Sulaiman (pbuh) consisted of Jinn, humans, and birds: 'And gathered for Solomon were his soldiers of the jinn and men and birds...' (17). If Jinn had meant humans, there would have been no need to mention Jinn separately here.

'They made for him what he willed of elevated chambers, statues, bowls like reservoirs, and stationary kettles. [We said], "Work, O family of David, in gratitude." And few of My servants are grateful.' (13)

This is a detail of the works in which Hazrat Sulaiman employed these subjected Jinn. All these works are constructional, cultural, and reformative. When the Jews fell into low sciences (sorcery) during their period of decline, they attributed these superstitions to Hazrat Sulaiman (pbuh). Here, the Qur'an clarifies that the knowledge through which Hazrat Sulaiman subjected the Jinn was bestowed upon him by Allah Almighty out of His special favor; it had nothing to do with low sciences, and the works he took from the Jinn were all constructional and cultural works. He did not use them for corrupt purposes. The detail of this theme has already passed in Surah Al-Baqarah under the verse: 'And Solomon did not disbelieve...' (102).

'of elevated chambers and statues': That is, Hazrat Sulaiman (pbuh) had these Jinn construct elevated chambers (maharib) and statues (tamathil). 'Mihrab' (plural: maharib), in my view, is used here in its well-known sense (arched chambers). The reason for its specific mention is that the most prominent part of any building is its arched chambers, which are the first to catch the eye of the observer. Therefore, the greatest display of architectural art is upon them. The details of the construction of the Temple and his palace among the buildings constructed by Hazrat Sulaiman (pbuh) are present in the Book of Kings. It is learned from it that the complete perfection of architectural art was spent on their arched chambers. Highly beautiful floral patterns were carved upon them.

'statues': 'Tamathil' is the plural of 'timthal'. 'Timthal' refers to a painted or engraved form, image, likeness, or a statue/sculpture of something. This form can be of non-living things—such as a river, mountain, tree, bush, flower, etc.—and it can also be of real or imaginary living things—such as humans, angels, Paradise, and animals, etc. From the Book of Kings in the Torah, it is learned that Hazrat Sulaiman (pbuh) had both of these types of statues made. For instance, in the description of his palace:

'And on the borders that were between the ledges were lions, oxen, and cherubim...' (1 Kings 7:29). Similarly, in the description of the construction of the Temple:

'In the inner sanctuary he made two cherubim of olivewood, each ten cubits high. Five cubits was the length of one wing of the cherub, and five cubits the length of the other wing...' (1 Kings 6:23-24).

'And the cedar within the house was carved with gourds and open flowers...' (1 Kings 6:18).

'He carved all the walls of the house round about with carved figures of cherubim and palm trees and open flowers, in the inner and outer rooms.' (1 Kings 6:29).

As far as the forms and statues of non-living things are concerned, there is no difference of opinion regarding their permissibility. However, regarding living things, particularly the statues of angels, it is difficult to understand how Hazrat Sulaiman (pbuh) considered it permissible. If the answer is given—as our commentators have generally done—that these things were not forbidden in the Shariah of the Children of Israel, then this answer is based on unfamiliarity with the Torah. In the Torah, the prohibition of these things is mentioned in highly clear words. In Exodus 20:2-5, it is stated:

'I am the Lord your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of slavery. You shall have no other gods before me. You shall not make for yourself a carved image, or any likeness of anything that is in heaven above, or that is in the earth beneath, or that is in the water under the earth. You shall not bow down to them or serve them, for I the Lord your God am a jealous God...'

See, the prohibition of making any image or likeness is mentioned in highly clear words. For this reason, the idea is incorrect that these things were permissible in previous Shariahs and were only declared forbidden in Islam. These things were forbidden before as well, and it cannot be assumed regarding Hazrat Sulaiman (pbuh) that he violated any command of the Torah. Therefore, our view is that he must have had only such statues made which relate to pure art and in which there was no trace of religious sanctity. But when the custom of image worship became prevalent among the Jews, their kings must have had such things made, and to give them a certificate of permissibility, they must have attributed them to Hazrat Sulaiman (pbuh). After all, the Jews also attributed all the low sciences (sorcery) to Hazrat Sulaiman (pbuh), which was refuted in Surah Al-Baqarah. Similar superstitions were also attributed to him in the Book of Kings. It should be clear that the Jews did not present Hazrat Sulaiman (pbuh) as a prophet, but as a purely worldly king, and they tried to tarnish his character from every aspect.

'bowls like reservoirs, and stationary kettles': 'Jifan' is the plural of 'jafnah', which means a large bowl or platter, and 'jawab' is the plural of 'jabiyah', which means a reservoir. 'Rasiyat' comes as an attribute for a mountain (firmly anchored). Here, it is used for those large kettles/cauldrons which were so heavy and massive that they could not be easily moved from one place to another. They remained permanently installed on stoves in one place, and food was cooked in them by the maunds (large quantities) at a single time.

In the preceding clause, the artistic aspect of the Sulaimanic culture was highlighted. In this clause, his generosity is highlighted—that the Jinn made large bowls for Hazrat Sulaiman (pbuh) which were like reservoirs, and massive cauldrons that remained anchored in one place. This mention of bowls like reservoirs and massive cauldrons is to express the generosity of Hazrat Sulaiman (pbuh). If you have to say that such and such person is highly generous, that a vast creation is nourished from his table of generosity, then in eloquent Arabic, these two words are sufficient to express it: 'he has stationary kettles.' Arab poets have used this very same metaphor for Hatim and their other generous figures.

From this, it is learned that the culture that came into existence at the hands of Hazrat Sulaiman did not consist merely of the display of science, art, and glory, but side by side with it, it also contained a highly vast and generous arrangement for the care of the poor. It is on this basis that Allah Almighty praised this culture. A culture in which both of these aspects are present in complete balance is a blessed culture. On the contrary, a culture in which there is the display of art and pomp, but the poor starve, is a satanic culture.

'[We said], "Work, O family of David, in gratitude."': This states the right of the favor and bounty that Allah Almighty bestowed upon Hazrat Sulaiman (pbuh). They were instructed that upon receiving this knowledge, science, and these earthly and heavenly blessings, do not go astray, but rather, with gratitude to your Lord, use everything in its correct place and take every step in the correct direction. Although every blessing of Allah Almighty gives this advice through the language of state, Hazrat Sulaiman (pbuh) was a prophet, so Allah Almighty instructed him regarding it through revelation as well. This is mentioned repeatedly in both the Psalms and Proverbs. Here, in the address to the 'family of Dawud', there is a reminder for Hazrat Sulaiman (pbuh), his family, and all his followers to follow in the footsteps of their father in showing gratitude to God, because all this greatness and glory they inherited from him, and Allah had bestowed all this upon him as a reward for his gratitude.

'And few of My servants are grateful': This is an additional and highly important warning—that the path of gratitude is not an easy path, but a highly difficult one. Allah Almighty bestows His blessings upon many, but those who fulfill their right upon receiving them turn out to be very few. Most turn out to be those who become rebellious and disobedient to God. From this, a point of the wisdom of religion is clarified: that between patience (sabr) and gratitude (shukr), the test of gratitude is the more difficult test. This is also supported by the statements of Hazrat Isa (Jesus). He clarified this reality by saying that it is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for a rich man to enter the kingdom of God.

'And when We decreed for him death, nothing indicated his death to them except a creature of the earth eating his staff. But when he fell, it became clear to the jinn that if they had known the unseen, they would not have remained in the humiliating punishment.' (14)

With the preceding verses, those who were in the misconception that all the undertakings of Hazrat Sulaiman (pbuh) were due to the favor of the Jinn were completely refuted. Now, at the end, it is also pointed out that the Jinn do not possess any means of knowing the unseen. They are as unaware of it as other creations. To point to this reality, reference is made to the event of Hazrat Sulaiman's (pbuh) death—that when Hazrat Sulaiman (pbuh) passed away, he was supervising the work of his subjected Jinn, but the Jinn did not get news of his death. They continued in their forced labor as usual. Ultimately, after a long period, they came to know that Hazrat Sulaiman (pbuh) had passed away, and only then could they obtain release from his servitude.

The event seems to have occurred in this manner: Hazrat Sulaiman (pbuh) used to supervise his important works, especially those performed by the Jinn, in person. Thus, he was supervising some construction work while leaning on his staff, and during this time, the hour of his death arrived and the angel of death took his soul. However, he remained standing as he was, supported by his staff, and the Jinn continued with their work out of fear, thinking that Hazrat Sulaiman (pbuh) was present. Finally, a long time passed, and during this interval, termites ate the staff from below, after which his blessed body fell to the ground. Only then did the Jinn realize that if they had possessed knowledge of the unseen, they would not have remained in the humiliation of this forced labor for so long.

'a creature of the earth': From the context in which it is mentioned here, it is understood that this refers to termites. 'Minsah' means a staff. Although it is not specified here how long Hazrat Sulaiman remained standing in this state, neither is the body remaining in this position highly improbable, nor is termites eating the staff in this manner improbable. Termites are a highly destructive thing. If they infect something, they finish it off very quickly; a staff is a very ordinary thing, particularly in places where they are abundant. Moreover, here the nature of the matter was also different. The will of Allah was that Hazrat Sulaiman's (pbuh) death should occur in such a way that it becomes clear to the people that Hazrat Sulaiman (pbuh), who possessed control over the wind and the Jinn, could not save himself from sudden death, and the delusion should also leave the minds of the Jinn that they know or can know the unseen. To clarify these realities, Allah Almighty gave this form to the death of Hazrat Sulaiman (pbuh), and Allah can do whatever He wills in whatever manner He pleases.

'it became clear to the jinn...': This points to the fact that the rebellious Jinn have always been in search of the unseen, and for this purpose, they used to sit in the heavens for eavesdropping, as is clear from Surah Al-Jinn and other places in the Qur'an. They had also established this leverage over the humans who fell into their trap—claiming that they possessed means to be aware of the secrets of the unseen. But this event opened their eyes and made it clear to them that let alone the heavens, they could not even get news of such a major event occurring right over their heads, due to which they had to remain captured in the humiliating punishment of servitude for an additional period.

From this clause, it is also learned that Hazrat Sulaiman (pbuh) had employed only the rebellious Jinn in his forced labor, and his knowledge of subjection was related only to them.

'There was for [the tribe of] Saba' in their dwelling place a sign: two [fields of] gardens on the right and on the left. [They were told], "Eat from the provision of your Lord and be grateful to Him. A good land and a Forgiving Lord."' (15)

The mention of 'Saba' has passed under verse 22 of Surah An-Naml. The region that is now called Yemen was formerly the region of Saba. This was a highly fertile and lush region. Along both sides of its main highway lay a series of lush gardens that spread over the entire region. But its inhabitants did not appreciate this favor of Allah Almighty, as a retribution for which Allah sent a flood upon them, destroying the entire country. Above, the character of Hazrat Dawud and Hazrat Sulaiman (peace be upon them) was presented as the character of grateful servants. Now, the character of the ungrateful and their end is being presented. In verse 112–113 of Surah An-Nahl, the Quraysh were also urged to take warning from their example.

'There was for [the tribe of] Saba' in their dwelling place a sign...': 'Saba' here refers to the people of Saba. 'Dwelling place' (maskan) is in the sense of region and territory. The indefiniteness (tankeer) of the word 'ayah' (sign) is to magnify its status. The meaning is that for the people of Saba, there was a very great sign of God's mercy, lordship, and His favor and bounty in their region, but they did not appreciate it. They did not learn the lesson from it which they were supposed to learn.

'two [fields of] gardens on the right and on the left': This is the explanation of that sign—that they had two rows of gardens on their right and left. From this, it is learned that their central highway passed through the middle of the region, and on both sides of this highway lay rows of gardens. 'Jannatan' (two gardens) here does not mean literally only two gardens, but two rows of gardens. Examples of this dual usage are present in Arabic, and its context here is clear.

'"Eat from the provision of your Lord and be grateful to Him"': This is the lesson that is derived from this great sign of God, and which the people of Saba were supposed to learn—that this great mercy and prosperity, through its language of state, is teaching them to benefit from this great table of their Lord's generosity and to remain grateful to Him.

'"A good land and a Forgiving Lord"': 'Good' (tayyibah) here is in the sense of fertile and lush. In this sense, this word is used in various places in the Qur'an. This is a further extension of the same lesson—that if they had looked at their country with open eyes, seeing this vast table of generosity upon which blessings were laid out for them with such abundance, this reality would have also become clear to them: that the One Who laid out this table of generosity is a highly Forgiving and Veiling Master, Who, without any entitlement on their part, and despite their disregard and ungratefulness, has piled up these blessings for them. We have been clarifying in various places in this book that the consciousness of gratitude to the Benefactor upon receiving a blessing is a self-evident requirement of human nature. This is even inherent in the instinct of animals. If a human is devoid of this consciousness, they are worse than animals.

'But they turned away [in rebellion], so We sent upon them the flood of the dam, and We replaced their two gardens with two gardens of bitter fruit, tamarisk, and something of sparse lote trees.' (16)

'Arim': Some lexicographers have written that it means heavy rain, and some have stated that it is the plural of 'arimah', which is used for stones piled layer upon layer. Then, from this, it also began to be used for that barrier or dam constructed in the middle of a valley to block water. In *Aqrab al-Mawarid*, it is: 'the dam built in the middle of a valley'—just like our Mangla and Tarbela dams. It is known from history that the people of Saba also had a similar dam, several miles long and wide, which protected them from the dangers of floods and from which they also obtained water as per requirement for irrigation. In history, it is mentioned by the name of the Dam of Ma'rib. Ma'rib was the capital of Saba. This dam broke probably in 542 AD, and with its breaking, the entire country was destroyed.

'But they turned away [in rebellion], so We sent upon them the flood of the dam': That is, when Allah Almighty gave them these blessings, instead of being grateful to God upon receiving them, they fell into rebellion and corruption. From verse 113 of Surah An-Nahl, it is learned that Allah sent a messenger to warn them, but they did not care. Finally, God's punishment came upon them in the form of a great flood that broke their dam, destroying their entire country.

'and We replaced their two gardens...': 'Khamt' refers to bitter, unpleasant, and low-quality fruits. That is, this flood turned their country upside down in such a way that in place of the lush gardens, there remained only a few trees of bitter, unpleasant fruits, some tamarisks, and wild berry bushes. This shows that the flood not only devastated their gardens, but also deposited a layer of soil or sand over their entire region which changed the nature of the remaining trees, leaving the entire region suitable only for wild bushes.

'That We repaid them because they disbelieved. And do We reward [i.e., punish] any but the ungrateful?' (17)

'Mujazat' means to give a recompense. Recompense can be equal, and it can be good. Therefore, its correct meaning is determined by the context. Here, the context is of a bad recompense (punishment); hence, the word will be taken in this sense here. It states: 'We gave them this punishment as a retribution for their ungratefulness for Our favors, and We do not give such punishments to anyone except the ungrateful.'

'And We placed between them and the cities which We had blessed [many] visible cities. And We determined between them the [distance of] journey, [saying], "Travel between them by night or day in safety."' (18)

'the cities which We had blessed' refers to the land of Syria, etc., with which the people of Saba had commercial relations. These lands were highly fertile; hence, they are described with the attribute: 'which We had blessed'. Commercial relations with fertile lands are themselves a source of income and prosperity.

'visible cities' (qura zahirah) refers to those towns and villages that were situated on the main highway, along both sides of which lay rows of gardens. That is, in addition to the settlements situated in the interior of the country, there were also settlements inhabited at suitable distances on this highway which served as stations for them. They would stay, rest, and then travel to the next station without any fear or danger.

'And We determined between them the [distance of] journey': That is, these settlements were situated at such suitable distances that it was as if nature itself had determined the stations for them by its own arrangement.

'"Travel between them by night or day in safety"': This is an expression of these comforts through the language of state—that nature, by making this entire arrangement, as if placed a sign at every station for them, saying: 'We have made this arrangement for your sake so that you may travel by night and by day without any fear or danger.' Here, a clause is omitted: 'and remain grateful to your Lord Who made this entire arrangement for you.' The reason for omitting it is that this is the natural requirement of every favor, and it has already been mentioned above in verse 15. Therefore, instead of repeating it, their attitude which they adopted and as a result of which they were deprived of all these blessings is pointed out in the subsequent verse.

Here, the reality should be kept in view that Allah Almighty has attributed the arrangement of all these comforts and prosperities directly to Himself. This is a statement of the actual reality—that whatever victories man achieves in this world, he achieves them only through the favor of Allah Almighty. But man, due to his ungratefulness, begins to consider everything as the miracle of his own effort and planning. This lack of insight is the primary cause of the general deviation of this era, and the inventions of science have increased this arrogance to such an extent that only Allah can now remove it.

'But [insolently] they said, "Our Lord, lengthen the distance between our journeys," and wronged themselves, so We made them narrations and dispersed them in total dispersion. Indeed in that are signs for everyone patient and grateful.' (19)

This is an expression of their language of state, not their spoken language—that upon receiving these prosperities, the attitude they adopted showed that they were not worthy of these comforts, but rather deserved that their settlements be ruined, their stations become difficult, and all these prosperities be stripped from them. There is no need to quote examples of expressions through the language of state; its precedents have passed, and we have also clarified the reality that the primary testimony is that of the language of state, not of spoken words. The statement of the Jews, 'We hear and we disobey,' is also an expression of their state.

'and wronged themselves': That is, they showed ungratefulness and disobedience to God, and in this way, they wronged their own souls by inviting the wrath and anger of God. They did not harm God in the least.

'so We made them narrations and dispersed them in total dispersion': This describes the end of their ungratefulness—that ultimately they were so trampled that they were wiped off the page of the present and remained only as a story of the past. 'And We dispersed them in total dispersion'—meaning those who survived the calamity of the flood were also completely scattered due to the destruction of the country's economy. Some went here, and some went there.

'Indeed in that are signs for everyone patient and grateful': And just as in verse 17, attention was drawn to the primary lesson of this story through the words: 'And do We reward any but the ungrateful?', similarly here, attention is drawn to the same reality through the words: 'Indeed in that are signs for everyone patient and grateful.' If you reflect, you will find that the story of the people of Saba is repeated twice here, and both times attention is drawn to its warning end. First, after mentioning the fertility and lushness of their region, their ungratefulness and its end was pointed out; then, after mentioning their cultural and commercial developments, their dispersion as a result of their ungratefulness was pointed out. This style of expression is adopted so that the primary objective for which this story is being narrated does not fade from view. There are several highly eloquent examples of this style of expression in the Qur'an.

'Patience' (sabr) and 'gratitude' (shukr) are counterparts. Both of them are required at the same time. The one who does not possess patience cannot fulfill the right of gratitude, and the one who does not possess gratitude cannot observe patience. In this world of trial, at every step, the servant's test is carried out in both of these things, and upon this test depends their success or failure in the Hereafter. It states that in this story, there are many signs for those who observe patience and gratitude. For example:

* Whatever blessings are achieved in this world are bestowed by Allah Almighty alone; therefore, upon receiving them, instead of falling into arrogance, man must remain grateful and obedient to his Lord.

* Whatever blessings are received are received not on the basis of any entitlement, but as a trial. Allah Almighty gives them to see whether His servants remain grateful or show ungratefulness.

* The nations that fail in this trial face its end in this world as a nation. In the Hereafter, everyone will face their end individually.

'And Iblis certainly found true his assumption about them, so they followed him, except for a party of believers.' (20)

This points to the assumption of Iblis which he had expressed before Allah Almighty when he was commanded to prostrate to Adam (pbuh) and he refused to comply. At that time, he had said: 'I will so surround the progeny of Adam that the majority of them will follow me instead of You, and You will not find most of them grateful' (Al-A'raf: 17). The objective of referring to it here is to point to their end—that when they proved Iblis's assumption about themselves to be true, its end also necessarily came and will come upon them. That is, in this world, they faced the punishment of the flood, and in the Hereafter, they will be cast into the punishment of Hell. Wherever this threat of Iblis is mentioned in the Qur'an, it is accompanied by this reply from Allah Almighty: 'Whoever follows you among them, I will surely fill Hell with you all.'

'except for a party of believers': We have already mentioned with reference to verse 113 of Surah An-Nahl that Allah Almighty had also sent a messenger to the people of Saba. From this verse, it is clear that their majority denied that messenger. Only a few people believed in him, and they alone remained safe from the punishment that came upon their nation.

'And he had over them no authority except [that We willed] that We might make evident who believes in the Hereafter from who is thereof in doubt. And your Lord, over all things, is Guardian.' (21)

This is a refutation of a potential objection, i.e., removing a doubt at the very outset, so that no one remains in the misconception that Satan possesses any authority over people to guide or misguide whomever he wills. It states that Allah Almighty has not given Satan any such authority over humans. He has only been given respite to invite people to the path of evil, and this respite has been given to him because Allah Almighty wants to test people: who possesses such a strong faith in the Hereafter that they remain firm on the truth despite Satan's incitments, and who is in doubt so that they follow his path upon his temptation. Before 'lina'lama' (that We might make evident), a verb in the sense of 'We saw' or 'We gave respite' is omitted, and examples of such omissions have passed before.

'And your Lord, over all things, is Guardian': That is, Allah Almighty, after sending Satan and man into this arena of trial, is not sitting aside, but is supervising everything. It is impossible for Satan to exceed his limits, and it is impossible for man to be deprived of any of his rights. If man tries to fulfill his duty to the extent of his capacity, Allah Almighty will help him in this world and will fully reward his every effort in the Hereafter. From this, it is learned that it is not permissible to abandon one's duty out of despair over the dominance of Satan and his progeny. Allah Almighty has indeed given respite to Satan, but He has not handed over His world to him; rather, He Himself is supervising everything.

4. Subsequent Theme: Verses 22–27

In the preceding verses, the reality was clarified that all mercy and punishment are in the power of Allah alone, and therefore, He alone is the true deserving recipient of gratitude and worship. Now, next, the polytheists are challenged: if you have any proof in favor of your deities whom you have made partners of God and because of whose hope of intercession you are carefree regarding God's grasp, then present it. At the same time, this declaration is made through the tongue of the Prophet (pbuh): 'If you do not possess any proof and are merely clinging to your view out of stubbornness and defiance, then we do not want to waste our time in debating with you, but rather leave the matter to Allah. He is the best of judges.' Recite the verses in this light.

5. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

'Say, "Invoke those you claim besides Allah." They do not possess an atom's weight within the heavens or on the earth, and they have therein no partnership [with Him], nor is there for Him from among them any assistant.' (22)

This is an open challenge to the polytheists: 'Call those whom you have assumed as partners of God, let us also see who and where they are!' Such a challenge is given to the addressee when their claim is entirely baseless and proof has been fully established against them from every aspect. This same style of address is in verse 27 below: 'Say, "Show me those whom you have attached to Him as partners." No! Rather, He is Allah, the Exalted in Might, the Wise.'

'They do not possess an atom's weight within the heavens or on the earth': This is a further explanation of this challenge—that you have assumed them as partners of God, whereas they do not possess even an atom's weight of authority in the heavens or the earth. This same point was clarified in the very first verse of this Surah: 'To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth' (1).

'and they have therein no partnership [with Him]': That is, they have no share in the creation of either of them. In another place, He says: 'I did not make them witnesses to the creation of the heavens and the earth...' (Al-Kahf: 51)—meaning, I did not call any of them at the time of the creation of the heavens and the earth to assist Me in the least.

'nor is there for Him from among them any assistant': That is, even in the management and administration of the heavens and the earth, God has not made any of them His assistant or helper, but rather He alone manages His entire kingdom, and this is not in the least a burden upon Him. This is a refutation of the illusion of the polytheists who thought that since the earth is a highly distant region of God's kingdom, He has entrusted its administration to His other partners.

'And intercession does not benefit with Him except for one whom He permits. [And those followers will continue in fear] until, when terror is removed from their hearts, they will say [to one another], "What has your Lord said?" They will say, "The truth." And He is the Most High, the Grand.' (23)

After negating ownership and authority, this negates their assumed concept of intercession as well. It states that if you are sitting complacently on the hope of their intercession, then remember that no one's intercession will benefit anyone before God except for the one for whom God gives permission. That is, first of all, no one will dare to intercede without God's permission, and even after permission, they will intercede only for the one for whom permission has been granted to them. They will not be able to open their mouth for anyone for whom permission has not been granted. In other places, the clarification is also given that they will say only that which is the truth. Before the Knower of the Unseen, no one will be able to turn falsehood into truth, nor will they be able to utter anything contrary to the truth.

This is a refutation of the assumption of the polytheists regarding their angel deities—that they are the beloved daughters of God, and therefore, they can get whatever they want accepted by their Father, and God has to accept their every word out of affection for them.

'until, when terror is removed from their hearts, they will say, "What has your Lord said?" They will say, "The truth"': 'Tafreegh' lexically means to frighten and terrify, but when followed by the preposition 'an', it means to remove terror. 'FuZZi'a 'an qulubihim' means: 'when terror is removed from their hearts.'

This describes the state of the angels on the Day of Judgment—that on that Day, let alone stepping forward to intercede with affection and familiarity, such terror will be cast upon them that they will have no news of what command was issued from the court of the Lord of Honor regarding the people. When their terror is removed, they will ask others: 'What command was issued from your Lord?' They will reply: 'An entirely appropriate command has been issued.'

Refuting this very concept of the polytheists regarding the angels, it is stated in Surah Az-Zumar:

'They have not appraised Allah with true appraisal, while the earth entirely will be within His grip on the Day of Resurrection, and the heavens will be folded in His right hand. Exalted is He and high above what they associate with Him. And the Horn will be blown, and whoever is in the heavens and whoever is on the earth will fall dead except whom Allah wills. Then it will be blown again, and at once they will be standing, looking on.' (Az-Zumar: 67-68)

These matters relate to the events of the Resurrection. Their correct imagination is not possible in this world, but both of these verses belong to the same context. Therefore, if the meaning of the verse under discussion is understood in the light of the aforementioned verse of Surah Az-Zumar, it emerges that when the Horn is blown the first time, all creations of the heavens and the earth will fall unconscious. Only those will remain safe from it whom Allah Almighty wills to keep safe. The mention of these people appears in another place as: 'The greatest terror will not grieve them' (Al-Anbiya: 103). Then, when the Horn is blown the second time, all people will stand up and look with wonder and terror at what has happened and what is about to happen! It seems that at this stage, the angels will ask the question which is mentioned here with the words: 'What has your Lord said?'. This question expresses their anxiety and terror—that let alone stepping forward to intercede with confidence and trust, the situation will be such that they will not even have their own senses fully intact. They will ask others what command has been issued from the divine court. Others, in reply, will only say that whatever command has been issued is entirely the truth. 'Al-Haqq' is in the accusative case (mansoob) due to an omitted verb. This reply can be from those whose matters will be decided on that Day, because on that Day the truth will become so manifest that no one will dare to say that any injustice was done to them. It is also possible that this reply is from those who will be kept safe from the terror of that Day, and who were pointed out in the aforementioned verse of Surah Az-Zumar with the words: 'except whom Allah wills'. If this reply is assumed to be from those people, it means that on that Day, the majority will be in such a state that they will be concerned only with themselves, so how could they intercede for others? And those who will be kept in safety from the terror of that Day will be in such a state that they will proclaim that whatever decision the Just and Generous Lord has made is entirely the truth. The meaning is: when such is the actual reality, who are those upon whose intercession the polytheists are relying?

'And He is the Most High, the Grand': That is, Allah's essence is highly elevated and grand, possessing great majesty and power. No one has the audacity to open their mouth before Him without His permission, nor does anyone possess such a stature as to have automatic access to Him.

Among the angels, Hazrat Jibreel (peace be upon him) holds the highest station, but regarding access to the court of Allah Almighty, even his helplessness is such that:

'If I fly even a hair's breadth higher, the glory of the manifestation will burn my wings.'

Further discussion on this issue will, God willing, follow in the next Surah.

'Say, "Who provides for you from the heavens and the earth?" Say, "Allah." And indeed, we or you are either upon guidance or in clear error.' (24)

That is, ask them: Who provides for you from the heavens and the earth? Who sends down water from the heavens, makes the sun shine, and creates seasonal changes? And who brings forth seeds from the earth, nurtures greens, and makes crops fruitful?

'Say, "Allah"': Tell them that Allah alone does all this, and this harmony of the heavens and the earth is a highly clear proof that a single will of the All-Powerful and Self-Sustaining God is operating in both of them.

Here, after asking the question, the answer is also given by Himself. This is an indication that there is no room for denial for the addressee. Regarding the beliefs of the Arabs, we have clarified in its proper place that they did not consider any of their deities as the creator or provider, but only as intercessors and means of proximity. It is clarified in the Qur'an that if you ask them: 'Who provides for you from the heavens and the earth?' they will immediately reply: 'Allah!' Since this matter was accepted by the addressee, Allah Almighty placed that very accepted reality before them.

'And indeed, we or you are either upon guidance or in clear error': The meaning is: our invitation is based on this very accepted reality. If you are disputing with us on this, then there is no need for further debate and dispute. Either we are on guidance and you are in clear error, or you are on guidance and we are in deviation. The meaning is: when you have resorted to such obstinacy that in a single breath you accept a reality and then deny it, then it is useless to continue further debate with you. Now your matter is left to Allah. He alone will decide who is on guidance and who is in deviation! In our view, this clause is in the sense of disassociation and finality, not in the sense of gentleness, as people have generally understood.

'Say, "You will not be asked about what we committed, and we will not be asked about what you do."' (25)

This is the same theme of concluding the argument that was in the preceding verse, expressed in other words. It states: if in your view we are guilty of opposing your deities and your ancestral customs, then before Allah the responsibility for this guilt lies upon us alone, not upon you. Similarly, we are not to be questioned about what you are doing; you alone will be questioned. Our responsibility was to deliver the truth, and we have fulfilled this duty. Now nothing is to be gained from further debate and dispute. You go your way, and we go ours. Tomorrow, the decision will be made in the presence of Allah Almighty as to who was on the truth and who was on falsehood. This same theme is stated in the following verse of Surah Ash-Shura:

'For us are our deeds, and for you are your deeds. There is no [need for] argument between us and you.' (15)

This same theme of concluding the debate is in Surah Al-Kafirun: 'For you is your religion, and for me is my religion' (6). This theme appears in various places in the Qur'an in different styles, and it has a specific context. That is: when the addressee, out of their obstinacy and defiance, tried to deny highly clear realities, they were told that now nothing is to be gained from debate and discussion; you do your work, and we do ours. People have generally taken such verses in the sense of expressing tolerance toward the addressee, but they are not in the sense of expressing tolerance, but rather in the sense of expressing disassociation and aversion. Due to not keeping the context in view, people fell into a misconception.

'Say, "Our Lord will bring us together; then He will judge between us in truth. And He is the Knowing Judge."' (26)

That is, tell them: if you are not ready to accept our word here, then do not accept it; a Day is about to come in which our Lord will gather us and you, and then He will make an entirely correct decision as to who was on the truth and who was on falsehood. He is a highly great Judge. No one will be able to resort to any obstinacy against His decision, and He possesses full knowledge of everything. Nothing is hidden from Him.

'Say, "Show me those whom you have attached to Him as partners." No! Rather, He is Allah, the Exalted in Might, the Wise.' (27)

The same theme that appeared in the first verse of this group of verses is stated again at the end in another style. Its tone is of sarcasm, contempt, warning, and threat. It states: tell them to show me those whom they have attached to God as partners! After all, who and where are they who hold such a high station as to be made partners in the kingdom of God!

'No! Rather, He is Allah, the Exalted in Might, the Wise': After asking the question, the answer is given by Himself: 'Never!' No one holds such a status or station as to be declared a partner of God. After this, proof of this negation is given from the accepted attributes of Allah Almighty. It states that He is 'Al-Aziz' (Exalted in Might) and 'Al-Hakeem' (Wise). 'Al-Aziz' means above everyone's reach and dominant over everything. 'Al-Hakeem' means His every action is based on wisdom. These attributes are mentioned in a restrictive style—meaning He is characterized by these attributes to the highest degree, and when He is characterized by them to the highest degree, there is no compatibility of shirk with them. This is because if someone is assumed as His partner from the aspect that God is in need of him in the creation and management of this universe, then this is a negation of His being 'Al-Aziz'; and if he is assumed from the aspect that some can affect His absolute justice through their reach and proximity, then this is a negation of His being 'Al-Hakeem'. It should be clear here that in the polytheism of the polytheists, these two misconceptions acted as the primary factors. Here, by referring to these two attributes of Allah Almighty, the foundation of both of these misconceptions was demolished.

6. Subsequent Theme: Verses 28–39

In the subsequent verses, the objections and demands of the opponents which they were presenting to deny the invitation of the Qur'an and its warnings are addressed. Answers to the objections and demands are given, and the end which those who deny the truth under the cover of these absurd objections and doubts will necessarily face is pointed out. Recite the verses.

7. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

'And We have not sent you except comprehensively to mankind as a giver of glad tidings and a warner. But most of the people do not know.' (28)

This is an expression of regret over the condition of those who, to deny the Qur'an's invitation to Tawhid and its warnings of punishment and Resurrection, demanded the punishment to harass the Prophet (pbuh). It states: 'We have not sent you to bring the punishment, but rather as a giver of glad tidings and a warner to the people'—so that you may give the glad tidings of salvation and mercy to those who believe in your invitation and adopt a life of faith and righteous deeds, and warn those of the wrath and anger of God which they will necessarily face in case of denying you. Your responsibility ends with this warning and glad tiding. Bringing the punishment or not bringing it is a matter that concerns Us. When its time comes, We will show this to them as well.

'But most of the people do not know': This is an expression of regret over the misfortune of the people—that Allah Almighty sent a warner before the punishment so that people, if they wish, could adopt a life of faith and righteous deeds to secure eternal mercy for themselves, but the condition of the people is such that they demand the punishment instead of mercy. They are not aware of the reality that when Allah Almighty sends a messenger to a nation, proof is fully established against them. If that nation denies the messenger, according to the divine law, they are necessarily destroyed. In the subsequent Surah—Surah Fatir, the explanation of this reality is given as follows:

'You are not but a warner. Indeed, We have sent you with truth as a giver of glad tidings and a warner. And there was no nation but that there had passed within it a warner. And if they deny you - then already have those before them denied. Their messengers came to them with clear proofs and written ordinances and the enlightening Scripture. Then I seized those who disbelieved, and how [terrible] was My reproach.' (Fatir: 23-26)

In this verse, the words 'kaffatan lin-nas' (comprehensively to mankind) are also noteworthy. The messengers who came before the Prophet (pbuh) came as warners only to their respective nations, but since the Prophet (pbuh) is the Seal of the Prophets and the Seal of the Messengers, his advent occurred to the entire creation. This reality has been clarified in the Qur'an from various aspects, and in the prophecies regarding him in the previous scriptures, this status of his is also prominent. We have quoted references to them in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah. The objective of reminding them of this here is to warn the People of the Book. During this phase of the invitation, they had also started covertly supporting the Quraysh. For this reason, by referring to this attribute of the Prophet (pbuh), they were also warned that the messenger whose opposition they are dedicating their intelligence and capability to has not come as a warner and giver of glad tidings only to the Quraysh, but rather their own fate is also to be decided through him. Therefore, if they wish to play this dangerous game, they should think through its consequences.

'And they say, "When is this promise, if you should be truthful?"' (29)

That is, instead of being warned by the messenger's warnings of punishment and thinking of saving themselves from it, they mock the messenger and his companions, and ask sarcastically: 'If you are truthful in your warning, then tell us when this threat will be manifest?' The meaning is: either show it to us or specify its time; otherwise, we consider it mere boasting.

'Say, "For you is the appointment of a Day [from which] you will not remain behind an hour, nor can you precede [it]."' (30)

It states: reply to them that do not hasten for this thing. A specific time is appointed for its manifestation. When that time comes, you will neither be able to draw back from it by an hour, nor will you be able to advance. We have clarified in various places that Allah Almighty gives respite to His disobedient and rebellious ones so that they may fully fill their cup, leaving them with no excuse, and only Allah Almighty knows when a nation's cup is filled. Even the messenger of Allah is not aware of this secret. The word 'Yawm' (Day) here is in the sense of time, and the word 'Sa'ah' (hour) is in the sense of a moment or a short duration. Within this style of expression, there is also a sarcastic reply to the sarcasm of the questioners—implying: on what strength do you want to know the time of this punishment? That inevitable time, when it comes, will not be averted by anyone's averting!

'And those who disbelieve say, "We will never believe in this Qur'an nor in that which was before it." And if you could see when the wrongdoers are made to stand before their Lord, refuting each other's words - those who were oppressed will say to those who were arrogant, "If not for you, we would have been believers."' (31)

People have given two interpretations for 'nor in that which was before it' (wa la billadhi bayna yadayh). First, that it refers to the heavenly scriptures revealed before the Qur'an. Second, that it points to the punishment and the Resurrection of which the Qur'an was warning them. In our view, this second view is more in harmony with the context. That is, these disbelievers, with great audacity, reply to the warnings of the Qur'an: 'Neither are we going to believe in this Qur'an, nor are we ready to accept the punishment and the Resurrection of which it is giving news.' Their meaning is: if we are to accept this, our demand is that this thing be shown to us. If it is not shown, then we are not going to fall for this threat!

'And if you could see when the wrongdoers are made to stand before their Lord...': 'Wrongdoers' (Zalimun) refers to these very unfortunate people who were rejecting God's mercy and demanding His punishment. The apodosis (jawab al-shart) is omitted here according to the well-known style of Arabic eloquence. This omission is adopted in places where the situation is beyond description and depiction. The meaning is: today these unfortunate people are denying the Qur'an and the subsequent Resurrection with great audacity, but if you could see their state when they are made to stand before their Lord, then... after this 'then', the depiction of the scene is omitted, indicating that its terror is beyond the limits of description.

'refuting each other's words' (yarji'u ba'duhum ila ba'dinil-qawl): The correct meaning is that they will bicker and argue with one another. Its detail is coming in the subsequent verses. The meaning is: today their leaders and followers are both united in opposing the Qur'an and the Resurrection. The leaders are at the forefront of opposition, and the common people are following them and holding their banners, but when they are presented before God, the followers will hold the leaders responsible for their deviation, and the leaders will blame the followers, saying: 'You are yourselves responsible for your ruin; why did you follow us instead of the truth after the truth had become clear?'
