The meaning is: today the leaders are convincing the common people to rely on their discretion, and that the responsibility of good and evil lies upon them, and the common people are following them blindly, thinking that if any danger arises, their leaders will save them from it. But when the actual stage comes, it will be known that everyone is responsible for their own deeds. No one will carry the burden of another.

'those who were oppressed will say to those who were arrogant, "If not for you, we would have been believers"': This explains the preceding statement. 'Those who were oppressed' (alladhina-stud'ifu) refers to those poor and common people who, due to their poverty and weakness, remained subordinate to the rich and became their tools. 'Those who were arrogant' (alladhina-stakbaru) refers to their wealthy leaders and chiefs who made themselves the standard of truth and falsehood, led the people behind them, and used them even for opposing Allah and the Messenger. It states that on that Day, the poor and common people will blame their leaders and wealthy figures: 'It is you because of whom we faced this misfortune; if you had not blocked our way, we would have certainly been among those who believed.' The meaning is: the truth was not hidden from us, and the urge to accept it was also arising within us, but your pressure suppressed our urge, and we were deprived of the blessing of faith against our wish, and ultimately reached this end.

'Those who were arrogant will say to those who were oppressed, "Did we avert you from guidance after it had come to you? Rather, you were criminals."' (32)

The leaders will immediately reply to their followers: 'Your accusation is entirely false—that we averted you from God's guidance; rather, you were yourselves criminals that you remained our followers even after highly clear guidance had come to you.' The meaning is: the truth was hidden neither from us nor from you. The messenger of Allah had fully established proof. If this had not occurred, then to some extent you would have been justified in blaming us. But when you stumbled in the full light of day, then instead of declaring us criminals, beat your own heads. We are criminals, and just like us, you are also criminals. Although this is said to the disbelievers, the Qur'an has quoted it as a true statement, and in it lies a lesson for the common people of every era—that in the matter of truth and guidance, no person can be exonerated before Allah merely on the excuse that others misled them; rather, the search for truth is everyone's own responsibility, and after it becomes clear, its following is obligatory upon every individual to the extent of their capacity. Without this, no one can be exonerated before Allah.

'And those who were oppressed will say to those who were arrogant, "Rather, [it was your] conspiracy by night and day when you were ordering us to disbelieve in Allah and attribute to Him equals." And they will disclose regret when they see the punishment; and We will put shackles on the necks of those who disbelieved. Are they recompensed except for what they used to do?' (33)

The common people will say in reply to the leaders: 'Rather, it was your conspiracies of day and night that deprived us of following the truth. You had launched a campaign of propaganda to turn us away from the messenger's guidance, and you used to teach and instruct us to remain firm on disbelief and polytheism.' From this reply, it emerged that they will admit that the truth was clear to them, but they could not follow it because the day and night conspiracies of their leaders deprived them of following it.

'And they will disclose regret when they see the punishment': 'Asarrun-nadama' (they will disclose/conceal regret) is an idiom of the same nature as 'abtanal-'adawa' (he concealed enmity). Regret is a state of the heart and mind; hence, 'asarru' (they concealed) is used for it. The objective is to show that after this bickering, when they see the punishment standing before them, they will be deeply remorseful of their misfortune. This shows that their excuse—that they were deprived of following guidance because of the conspiracies of their leaders—will not be accepted by Allah, but they will necessarily face the punishment. Their remorse will be because at that time it will become entirely clear to them that they followed their leaders against their own conscience. A person who follows falsehood against their own conscience, when its result comes before them, does not face only the bitterness of the result, but also the curse of their own conscience, and this double trouble increases their misery. This theme has passed in verses 66–68 of Surah Al-Ahzab. If further explanation is desired, have a look at it.

'and We will put shackles on the necks of those who disbelieved': It states: 'We will put shackles on the necks of those who disbelieved.' This points to the complete correspondence between the deed and its retribution. Those who completely suspend their intellect and conscience and hand over their reins to others, Allah Almighty will punish them on the Day of Judgment by putting shackles on their necks, by holding which the keepers of Hell will drag them into Hell.

'Are they recompensed except for what they used to do?': That is, putting these shackles on their necks will not be any injustice to them, but rather whatever they did in the world will appear before them in this form. They put the collar of others' servitude on their necks and disobied their Lord, as a result of which they will have to put these shackles of Hell on their necks.

'And We did not send into a city any warner except that its affluent said, "Indeed we, in that with which you were sent, are disbelievers."' (34)

This is said for the consolation of the Prophet (pbuh)—that what the chiefs and wealthy of your time are doing with you is the very same thing that the affluent of every era did with the messengers of their respective times. They also, with great arrogance, gave this reply to their messengers: 'We are deniers of the entire message with which you have been sent.' That is: neither are we ready to accept your invitation to Tawhid and faith, nor are we going to fall for your threat that if we do not accept your word, some punishment will come upon us.

Within the expression 'that with which you were sent' (bima ursiltum bihi), it is also implied: 'this messengership of which you are claiming is also merely your assumption, which has no reality in our view.'

'And they said, "We are more [abundant] in wealth and children, and we are not to be punished."' (35)

That is, the proof they presented to deny the warnings of the messengers was the very same proof that the affluent of your time are presenting. They replied to the messenger and his companions: 'You warn us that our belief and deed are false and that we deserve God's punishment, whereas the case is that we are far ahead of you in wealth and children. If your word were true, it required that you be ahead of us in wealth and children.' 'and we are not to be punished'—meaning, when the situation openly refutes you, we believe that you are merely boasting; neither is any punishment going to come upon us in this world, nor do we have any fear of punishment in the Hereafter (if it is to occur according to your assumption).

'Say, "Indeed, my Lord extends provision for whom He wills and restricts [it], but most of the people do not know."' (36)

It states: to remove this misconception of theirs, tell them that in this world, the abundance of wealth and children is neither a proof of being favored by God, nor is their shortage a proof of being contemptible in the eyes of God. Rather, this abundance and shortage depend entirely upon the choice of Allah Almighty and His wisdom. If He extends provision for someone, He tests their gratitude; and if He restricts provision for someone, He tests their patience. And actual success depends on the result of this test, which will appear in the Hereafter.

'but most of the people do not know': But most people are unaware of this reality. They fall into the foolishness of thinking that the wealth and status they possess is the miracle of their own capability and entitlement, and a proof of their being close and favored in the sight of God. In this way, they fall into the trap of Satan and turn God's blessing into a punishment for themselves.

'And it is not your wealth or your children that bring you near to Us in position, but only he who believes and does righteousness. For those there will be the double reward for what they did, and they will be in the upper chambers [of Paradise], safe.' (37)

'Zulfa' is a verbal noun (masdar) meant to emphasize the action—meaning: 'to bring you close in proximity.' The meaning is: wealth and children are not among those things that can be a cause of proximity to God in the least. Allah Almighty gives these things as a test to see whether the servant shows ungratefulness upon receiving them or adopts the path of faith and righteous deeds. Therefore, the means of proximity to God is not wealth and children, but faith and righteous deeds. If someone has not earned the wealth of faith and righteous deeds, then for them, the abundance of wealth and children will only add to their ruin.

'For those there will be the double reward for what they did': The word 'di'f' (double/multiple) in Arabic is used for the likeness of a thing and also for its multiple equivalents, no matter how unlimited those equivalents might be. For this reason, the meaning of this clause will be: for those who adopt the path of faith and righteous deeds, there will be a multiplied reward for their deeds. The explanation of this multiplied reward has occurred in other places in the Qur'an. We have clarified it in its proper place. Do not take it in the limited sense of only a double reward.

'and they will be in the upper chambers [of Paradise], safe': This points to the reality that in this world, there are constantly dangers for wealth and children, and no one can keep themselves safe from these dangers. But those who make their wealth and children a means of success in the Hereafter, the reward they will receive for it will be eternal and everlasting. In this regard, they will never face any anxiety again.

'And the ones who strive against Our verses to cause failure - those will be present in the punishment.' (38)

This describes the end of those who used the abundance of wealth and children as a proof to deny the verses of Allah and to harass the Messenger. It states: these people will be grabbed and brought into God's punishment to face the retribution for their pride and arrogance. The depiction of their disgrace and helplessness in the word 'muhdaroon' (brought/present) is obvious.

'Say, "Indeed, my Lord extends provision for whom He wills of His servants and restricts [it] for him. And whatever thing you spend - He will replace it; and He is the best of providers."' (39)

The introduction of this verse and the introduction of verse 36 above are exactly the same, but the objective in both places is different. In the verse above, it was clarified that the extension and restriction of provision depend entirely upon the choice of Allah Almighty; therefore, neither has anyone the right to boast over its extension, nor is it permissible for anyone to despair over its restriction; rather, everyone should keep the reality in view that both of these states occur under the wisdom of Allah Almighty, and through both, He tests the gratitude or patience of His servants. In this verse, the Prophet (pbuh) is instructed to tell these affluent figures that instead of boasting over the provision and favor bestowed by Allah, they should spend it in the works of God's pleasure, as this is the requirement of gratitude. And they should remain assured that whatever they spend in the way of God will not go to waste, but He will fully reward even the smallest spending. Above, in the matter of reward, the principle of 'jaza' ad-di'f' (multiplied reward) has been stated and explained. In the verse, after 'wama anfaqtum' (whatever you spend), the words 'in the way of Allah' are omitted due to contextual clarity. 'Akhlafa-llahu 'alayk' means: 'God returned to you what went away' (He replaced what you spent).

'and He is the best of providers': That is, those who spend in the way of God should remain assured that Allah Almighty is the best of providers. Today they cannot even imagine what He can and will bestow upon them tomorrow in exchange for the broken pieces of clay they spend. The word 'Khayr' (best) here is not in the sense of comparison and preference, but for the absolute description of the attribute. The explanation of this style has occurred in its proper place.

8. Subsequent Theme: Verses 40–45

Next, the affluent are warned: 'You are making the angels deities and worshipping them, thinking that if the stage of the Hereafter arises, they will save you; whereas in the Hereafter they will declare their disassociation from you, and no one will save you from God's punishment. Allah has bestowed a great favor upon you by sending a messenger to warn you, but due to your misfortune, you are calling his word magic. It is better for you to take warning from the end of the nations of the past. You have not achieved even a tenth of what they possessed, but when they were seized in God's grasp, no one could provide refuge to them.' Recite the verses.

9. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

'And [mention] the Day He will gather them all...': The emphasis of 'jami'an' (all together) shows the reality that on the Day of Judgment, the disbelievers and polytheists will be gathered, and to testify against them, Allah Almighty will also gather those whom they claimed to worship or to whom they attributed their innovations. In Surah Al-Ma'idah, the mention of the testimony of the Prophets (peace be upon them) has passed—that they will declare their disassociation before Allah Almighty from all those innovations which their inventing followers fabricated after them. Similarly, Allah Almighty will gather the angels and ask them: 'These polytheists (referring to the polytheists of Arabia) claim that they used to worship you; is this claim of theirs correct?' The purpose of this question, obviously, is to establish proof against the polytheists—that those whom they worshipped throughout their lives, considering them partners and intercessors, are neither aware of this worship of theirs, nor did they command them to do so, nor are they ready to tolerate for a single moment that anyone makes them partners of the Lord of the worlds and worships them.

'They will say, "Exalted are You! You are our benefactor [i.e., protector] not them. Rather, they used to worship the jinn; most of them were believers in them."' (41)

The angels will immediately reply to this question: 'Glory be to You! You are too pure and elevated to have any partner or associate!'

From this reply of the angels, one reality clarified is that polytheism is such a disgusting thing that upon hearing its mention, they will express their aversion to it and declare Allah to be high above having any partner.

Second, it is clarified that they will consider it a grave slander upon themselves that some wrongdoers tried to implicate them in the filth of polytheism.

Third, it emerged that those fools who did so carry the entire responsibility upon themselves. The angels are free from commanding anyone to worship them.

'"You are our protector, not them"': This will be the angels' appeal to their Lord—that against these wrongdoers who tried to implicate us in this crime, You are our Helper, Protector, and place of return. We are free from them; so clear us of their slander. 'Min dunihim' (besides them) is in the sense of 'against them'.

'"Rather, they used to worship the jinn; most of them were believers in them"': That is, this assumption of theirs is entirely false—that they used to worship us; rather, the actual fact is that these people used to worship the Jinn, because the majority of them believed in them.

This reply of the angels is entirely in accordance with the reality. This is because the idols they carved under the names of the angels—such as Lat, Manat, and Uzza, etc.—were merely names assumed by themselves, for which no actual entity existed. However, through their priests, they had established contact with the Jinn, and since they considered them a source of the knowledge of the unseen, they made offerings and sacrifices to them and called upon them. 'most of them were believers in them'—meaning they believed in them in the capacity that they bring news of the unseen and are themselves beneficial and harmful.

'So today none of you has power for another of benefit or harm, and We will say to those who wronged, "Taste the punishment of the Fire which you used to deny."' (42)

That is, after this reply of the angels, their worshippers will be told: 'You have heard the testimony of the angels, upon whom you placed all your reliance, in public. Therefore, on this Day, no one will be of any use to another. Neither can they bring any benefit or harm to you, nor can you to them.'

'and We will say to those who wronged...': 'Those who wronged' (alladhina zalamu) refers to the polytheists, who committed a grave injustice by making partners of God without any proof, and when the Prophet warned them of God's punishment, they denied it with great audacity. It states that after this final establishment of proof in the Hereafter, We will tell them: 'Now taste the fire of Hell which you used to deny.'

'And when Our clear verses are recited to them, they say, "This is not but a man who wishes to avert you from that which your fathers used to worship." And they say, "This is not except a fabricated lie." And those who disbelieve say of the truth when it has come to them, "This is not but obvious magic."' (43)

This is an expression of wonder over their stubbornness and defiance, and an expression of regret over their deprivation and misfortune—that they are trying to cast dust upon the sun. When Our highly clear, firm, and enlightening verses are recited to them, they incite their common people against them, saying: 'This man wishes to turn you away from those deities whom your noble ancestors continuously worshipped.' To incite the common people against realities, referring to the ancestors has always been an effective weapon in the hands of troublemakers; rather, if it is said, it would probably not be wrong, that this is the sole argument that the leaders of deviation have presented in favor of their deviations. The indefiniteness (tankeer) in 'ma hadha illa rajul' (this is not but a man) also contains a poison. That is, they try to convince their common people regarding the Prophet (pbuh): 'Do not fall for the trap of this man who is presenting himself before you as a messenger sent by God. He is merely an ordinary kind of man who has assumed this role only to divert you from your ancestral religion, claiming that God has sent him as a messenger to you.'

'And they say, "This is not except a fabricated lie"': This refers to their propaganda against the Qur'an—that they convince their common people: 'This claim made about this Qur'an, that Allah Almighty has sent it down, is entirely a lie made up only to overawe you. The actual fact is that this is a self-made book which is being falsely attributed to Allah Almighty.' 'And those who disbelieve say of the truth when it has come to them, "This is not but obvious magic"': This is an expression of wonder over their nonsense—that they are making these fabrications in opposition to the truth when the truth has already become manifest before them. If the sun is absent from the horizon and a stubborn person raises a question about it, this stubbornness is not highly surprising; but when the sun is shining at midday and someone calls it magic, this is sheer madness.

In 'lil-haqq' (of the truth), 'truth' refers to the Qur'an, and 'li' (for/of) is in the sense of 'fi' (in/regarding). Its usage in this sense is well-known. Examples of this have passed in this book.

'"This is not but obvious magic"': This is the detail of their statement—that they call this Qur'an magic. We have explained the calling of the Qur'an as magic in its proper place—that even its bitterest opponents admitted its miraculous eloquence, influence, and sway, but they wanted to convince their common people that this influence and sway was the miracle of its presenter's magical speech, having no relation to God and the angels.

'And We had not given them any scriptures which they could study, and We had not sent to them before you any warner.' (44)

This is an expression of favor upon these opponents, and an expression of regret over their disregard and deprivation. It states: these have been unlettered people, unaware of the Qur'an and scripture. Before this, neither had Allah given them any book which would serve as a source of teaching, study, and guidance for them, nor had any warner come to them before you to wake them from this sleep of negligence and warn them of the dangers ahead. The requirement of this situation was that they should have valued this great blessing with heart and soul, accepted it immediately, and with it reformed their world and also attained the kingdom of the Hereafter. But they are opposing it, and in this way, inviting that wrath of God which, according to the divine law, necessarily comes upon a nation that denies the warner sent to it.

After 'Kutub' (scriptures), the attribute 'yadrusunaha' (which they could study) points to the reality that until now they had not been given heavenly scriptures of the kind given to the Children of Israel, which they would study. The teaching they had received through Hazrat Ibrahim and Hazrat Ismail (peace be upon them) had taken the form of traditions, which with the passage of time had been almost forgotten, and the dominance of innovations had completely distorted their form. Allah Almighty willed to complete His favor upon them by giving them His Book, but their condition is such that they are calling it magic and fabrication.

'And those before them denied, and they [i.e., the people of Makkah] have not attained a tenth of what We had given them. And they denied My messengers, so how [terrible] was My reproach.' (45)

This is a warning to them—that denying God's messenger and His warner logically results in the destruction of that nation, and the entire human history testifies to this divine law.

'and they have not attained a tenth of what We had given them': Within the discourse, this is a highly fitting warning—that reference is being made to those nations who, in terms of pomp, glory, power, and abundance of resources, had reached a position that these rebels of the Quraysh do not reach even the dust of their feet. Yet, when they denied Allah's messengers, how God's curse fell upon them has been described in detail in the Qur'an. The meaning is: if they are proud of their resources, this pride is entirely baseless. Nations of great power and glory were blown away in a single breath of God, so what is their reality? They have not attained even a tenth of what those possessed. They have gone astray over very little due to their narrow-mindedness.

10. Subsequent Theme: Verses 46–54

Next, the concluding verses of the Surah contain a highly compassionate advice to the opponents, and also a highly powerful warning to alert them: 'Come to your senses. If this time passes, the lost time will not come back. At that time you will regret, but this regret will be entirely useless.' Recite the verses.

11. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

'Say, "I only advise you of one thing - that you stand for Allah, [seeking truth] in pairs and individually, and then reflect." There is not in your companion any madness. He is only a warner to you before a severe punishment.' (46)

It has passed under the preceding verses that when the Prophet (pbuh) warned the rebels of his nation of the punishment and the Resurrection, they imputed this statement of his to a kind of madness. They said that this man is afflicted with a kind of melancholia due to which he sees nothing but punishment all around. Replying to this in a highly advisory tone, He says: tell them that I tell you a single thing; for God's sake, act upon it. That is: clear your mind of obstinacy, prejudice, and pride, stand up in pairs and individually, listen to my words, and reflect on the matter with seriousness. 'Lillah' (for Allah) is meant to draw attention to the reality that until now your approach has been entirely subordinate to the self and Satan, due to which you could not reflect on the matter impartially. But the greatest right upon man is that of Allah Almighty, and you also believe in Allah; so for His sake, freeing yourselves from your other desires and motivations, stand up anew to reflect on the actual reality. 'Stand up'—meaning intend it and gird up your loins for it. In this atmosphere of prejudice, this thing will be heavy upon your hearts, but for Allah's sake, bear this heavy thing. This is the path of success.

With the restriction of 'in pairs and individually' (mathna wa furada), it is meant to draw attention to the reality that the mind of a crowd is not capable of thinking and understanding realities. Until now, your approach has been such that whenever any word came out of my mouth, your troublemakers tried to raise a clamor against it so that no one could listen to and understand my word. Abandon this method, and adopt instead the method of coming to me in pairs and individually, listening to my words with attention, reflecting on my proofs, presenting your doubts to find their answers from me, and then reflecting on whether what I am saying is said in madness, or if I am placing before you the end which you must necessarily face tomorrow.

'There is not in your companion any madness': That is, if you reflect with seriousness in this manner, this reality will become manifest to you that there is no madness in your companion, but rather a veil has fallen over your own intellects that you are forming a clique against him to harass him.

The word 'Sahib' (companion) here points to the reality that the person whom you are declaring crazy and mad is not someone new to you; rather, he was born among you, grew up before your eyes, and reached this age in front of you. In this entire period, you saw that he joined you in your every good deed and avoided your every evil. In every test, you found him truthful and trustworthy. You relied on his understanding, intellect, and insight, and considered him the wisest and most sensible among you; yet today, you are declaring this wise man crazy! Reflect: is he crazy, or are you!

Here, there is a pause after 'tatafakkaru' (then reflect), and this pause is highly eloquent. It is as if after inviting them to reflect, people were also given some time to reflect. After this, the correct result that should emerge from this reflection was placed before them. There are several examples of such pauses in the Qur'an.

'He is only a warner to you before a severe punishment': This statement is also highly fitting here. The meaning is: this which you see—that a single concern of the punishment and the Hereafter is always riding over his heart and mind, that at every moment of standing and sitting he warns you of it and advises you to prepare for it, and that despite all your disregard, offenses, and cruelties, he follows you—this restlessness is not because he is afflicted with some madness, but its primary cause is that he is seeing with his own eyes the punishment that is hovering over your heads, while you cannot see it. This entire anxiety and restlessness of his is in your sympathy, but due to your blindness, you impute this anxiety and restlessness of his to madness and melancholy.

It should be kept in mind here that for a messenger to see the punishment coming upon his nation is not a metaphor, but a reality. A messenger comes to his nation like a rain cloud of mercy, but within this very rain cloud of mercy is also hidden the thunderbolt of punishment for his nation if the nation denies him. This punishment serves as the introduction to the punishment of the Hereafter. For this reason, every messenger warned his nation of both of these punishments, and warned with such certainty as if he were seeing this punishment with both of his eyes. But those blind of intellect imputed this certainty of his to madness. The cry of 'Wa-sabahah!' (O, the disaster of the morning!) raised by the Prophet (pbuh) upon climbing Mount Safa was a demonstration of this very certainty, but the leaders of the Quraysh, instead of being warned by it, made fun of it.

'Say, "Whatever payment I might have asked of you - it is yours. My payment is only from Allah, and He is, over all things, Witness."' (47)

This theme appears in the Qur'an in two styles, and the import of both is the same. One style is: 'I have asked no reward of you for my service so that you are running away from me; my reward is only upon my Lord.'

The second is: 'If I have desired anything of you which you consider a reward and feel as a burden, then it is not for myself, but for your own good in this world and the Hereafter.'

Examples of the first style are abundant; there is no need to refer to them. Here, this theme is expressed in this second style, of which we quote some precedents. In Surah Al-Furqan:

'Say, "I do not ask of you for it any payment - only that whoever wills may take to his Lord a way."' (57)

Although this verse can also be interpreted in the sense of the first style, and that is also not contrary to Arabic usage, its meaning is also highly clear: 'I have desired nothing for myself from you for this service of mine. If I have desired anything, I have desired it only for you—that whoever among you seeks the way of God, I may show them God's straight path.'

Similarly, in Surah Ash-Shura:

'Say, "I do not ask you for it any payment except love among kinsmen."' (23)

Although this verse can also be interpreted in the light of the first style—which we will discuss in its proper place—its meaning is also highly clear: 'If you consider my invitation to spend and maintain family ties as a burden, then this also I desire not for myself, but for your own sake and for the sake of your own relatives and kinsmen.'

Based on this style, the verse under discussion states: 'First of all, I have demanded no reward from you for this invitation and teaching of mine; my reward is upon my Lord. But if you consider any of my demands as a burden upon yourselves and imagine it to be my reward, then this is your misconception and crooked thinking. Whatever I have desired of you, I have desired not for myself, but for you. When I warn you of God and the Hereafter, teach you goodness, and urge you to spend in the way of Allah, feed the poor and orphans, and free slaves, there is nothing in these things in which lies my personal interest; rather, their benefit, from both individual and collective aspects, will be achieved by you. By acting upon these things, you will reform your world and also make your Hereafter.'

Wherever this is said in the Qur'an, it is said in the context of reproaching the addressees and expressing disassociation from them. The meaning is: tell these ungrateful figures that I have not placed any ticket or fee upon my invitation so that it prevents you from coming to me and listening to my word; you can come to me at any time and without hesitation. There is no restriction on anyone in my gathering. My door is open and my chest is wide for both your rich and poor, and if you do not come, remember that you will not harm me in the least, but only yourselves. I have not opened a shop so that my shop will shut down due to your indifference. I received it free from my Lord, and I am distributing it free.

'and He is, over all things, Witness': That is, the Allah upon Whom lies my reward is present and watching over everything. I have no fear that even my smallest service will remain hidden from Him. He is watching my activities, and your disregard is also in His sight. I have full trust that He will fully reward me for my services.

'Say, "Indeed, my Lord projects the truth. [He is] Knower of the unseen."' (48)

After 'yaqdhifu bil-haqq' (He projects/hurls the truth), the words 'against falsehood' are omitted due to contextual clarity, as stated in another place: 'Rather, We hurl the truth against falsehood, and it knocks out its brains...' (Al-Anbiya: 18).

That is, tell these ignorant people not to remain in this misconception that this world was created in vain and will end in vain; rather, a Day is necessarily about to come when its Creator will judge between its truth and falsehood and hurl the truth against falsehood, due to which falsehood will be smashed to pieces. It should be kept in mind here that although this primarily relates to the supreme court of the Hereafter, this same result is also produced by that minor court which appears in the form of a messenger's advent. We have clarified in various places this divine law—that the nation to which a messenger was sent had its decision of truth and falsehood necessarily made. Thus, at the time of the Conquest of Makkah, the Prophet (pbuh) proclaimed: 'The truth has come, and falsehood has vanished. Indeed, falsehood is ever bound to vanish.'

'Knower of the unseen' (Allamul-ghuyub): In our view, this is an independent clause, and the subject (mubtada) is omitted here. This presents proof of the judgment between truth and falsehood from the divine attributes—that the Lord of this universe knows all unseen matters, nothing is hidden from Him, and therefore, let no one remain in the misconception that He will face any difficulty in deciding any matter. He will place everyone's complete register of deeds before them, in which their every deed will be recorded, no matter in how hidden corners it was performed.

'Say, "The truth has come, and falsehood can neither originate [anything] nor repeat [it]."' (49)

Just as in the preceding verse, after 'yaqdhifu bil-haqq', the words 'falsehood vanished' are omitted, similarly here, after 'ja'al-haqq' (the truth has come), the words 'and falsehood vanished' are omitted, and this gap has been filled by the words: 'and falsehood can neither originate [anything] nor repeat [it]'.

It states: tell these ignorant people that now the truth has come in the form of the Qur'an, and when the truth has come, the destruction of this falsehood which they have been worshipping is inevitable. Now, if they remain clung to this falsehood, they will also be crushed with it.

'and falsehood can neither originate [anything] nor repeat [it]': This presents proof of the transience of falsehood—that the Creator of this workshop of the universe initiated it with truth; therefore, it is necessary that its end is also upon truth. When falsehood has no share in its origination, how can it have a share in its repetition? Therefore, those who are spending their lives relying on the intercessions of imaginary deities should remember that their deities possess authority neither in the origination of anything nor in its repetition. It should be kept in mind here that the polytheists of Arabia did not associate anyone in the origination of this universe, but they believed that if the stage of the Hereafter arose, their return would be to their deities who would secure high status for them there through their intercessions. To remove this misconception, the Qur'an repeatedly took them to task for this confusion of theirs—that when you do not associate anyone in the origination of this universe, why do you associate them in its repetition?

In Surah Yunus, this same theme is expressed as follows:

'To Him is your return all together. [It is] the promise of Allah which is indeed true. Indeed, He begins the creation; then He repeats it that He may reward those who believed and did righteous deeds in justice.' (Yunus: 4)

Then this theme is stated in another style as follows:

'Say, "Is there of your partners [those] who begins creation and then repeats it?" Say, "Allah begins creation and then repeats it; so how are you deluded?"' (Yunus: 34)

'Say, "If I should go astray, I would only go astray against myself. And if I am guided, it is of what my Lord reveals to me. Indeed, He is Hearing and Near."' (50)

Now this warns them: tell them that if I am in deviation, as you think, then the consequence of this deviation is to be faced only by me, and you are safe from its ruin because you are not joining me in it. But there is another aspect to this issue, which you should also reflect on with seriousness. That is: if I am on guidance, then this guidance has been achieved by me due to this revelation which my Lord is sending down upon me. In this case, denying me is not an easy thing for you; rather, you turn out to be deniers of the divine revelation, and the end which those who deny the divine revelation must face is no ordinary thing. The meaning is: think through its results and consequences.

'Indeed, He is Hearing and Near': This is an expression of leaving the matter to Allah. The meaning is: now further debate and dispute with you is useless. I say this final word and leave your matter to my Lord. Neither are you ready to listen to my word, nor do you tolerate coming close; but my Lord is the One Who hears my words and is also near. So instead of wasting my energy behind you, I will make my supplications to Him Who hears and accepts.

'And if you could see when they are terrified but there is no escape, and they are seized from a place nearby.' (51)

Here, the apodosis (jawab al-shart) is omitted. Its example has passed in verse 31. This omission of the apodosis is proof that the answer is beyond description and depiction. It states: today their arrogance is such that they do not let anyone touch their shoulder, but if you could see their state when they are seized in that grasp of God which they are denying today, then you would have some idea of their helplessness—how they rub their noses and confess the reality. It states that when they are seized in the punishment of God, they will try to run away and escape in panic, but who has ever escaped from God's grasp so that they can run away from it? 'and they are seized from a place nearby'—meaning they will want to run away, but they will be grabbed from right nearby. From beneath their feet or from above their heads, God's punishment will seize them in such a way that they will not find any way of escape in any direction.

'And they will say, "We believe in it." But how for them is the reaching [of faith] from a place far off?' (52)

That is, at that time their intoxication will vanish and they will cry out: 'Now we believe in this Qur'an.' The pronoun (damier) here refers to the Qur'an, which was mentioned in the preceding verses. The one presenting the Qur'an, i.e., the Prophet (pbuh), is automatically included in it.

'But how for them is the reaching [of faith] from a place far off?': That is, the place of faith is this world, and that too before the manifestation of God's punishment. When God's punishment is manifest, at that time they will have gone so far from the station of faith that there will remain no possibility for them to attain it. After being seized in God's grasp, even a rebel like Pharaoh cried out: 'I believe in the Lord of Moses and Aaron!' But he received this reply from Allah Almighty: 'Now? And you had disobeyed before'—meaning: now you believe! whereas when it was the time to believe, you disobeyed.

'Tanawush' means to attain/reach, and 'anna lahum' means: how can there be any possibility for them to attain it at that time!

'The hand is short, and the dates are high on the palm tree.'

'And they had already disbelieved in it before, and they hurl [accusations] of the unseen from a place far off.' (53)

'Qadhf bil-hijarah' means to throw stones. 'Qadhf bil-qawl' means to speak without reflection and contemplation—meaning to throw a word without thinking. From this, it also comes in the sense of 'rajman bil-ghayb'—meaning to guess in the dark or speak at random. The meaning is: after seeing the punishment, they will confess faith, whereas before this, they sat at a distance and threw random guesses—claiming that this Book is self-made, its presenter is (God forbid) self-deluded, the threat of punishment is mere boasting, the warning of Resurrection is merely the result of a disordered mind, and that if any stage of Resurrection arose, our dominance would remain established there as well due to our partners. In short, keeping intellect aside and in the madness of denying the Qur'an and the Prophet (pbuh), they spoke whatever came to their tongue; but when the reality comes before them, they will confess it. However, this confession will be after the time has passed and will be entirely useless.

'And prevention will be placed between them and what they desire, as was done with their sects before. Indeed, they were in disquieting doubt.' (54)

That is, when the decisive punishment appears, a wall will be placed between them and all their desires. They will want to believe, but this desire of theirs will be useless. They will request respite, but no hearing will be given. They will call their partners, but no help will come from their side either. In short, all doors of hope will be closed upon them, and they will face an eternal despair.

'as was done with their sects before': That is, they will also have to face the same situation which their predecessors of the same conduct faced. This refers to 'Ad, Thamud, the people of Madyan, and Pharaoh, etc., whose stories have been narrated in detail in the Qur'an. The meaning is: these are not things which have not occurred before. History is witness that all those nations faced this very calamity who, like them, denied the warnings of their messengers. If they do not take warning from their states, they will necessarily face their end.

'Indeed, they were in disquieting doubt': That is, they also, like them, remained in a doubt that caused them anxiety, until finally the decisive hour arrived over their heads.

By the grace and tawfeeq of Allah, the commentary of this Surah is completed on these lines. All praise belongs to Allah for this.
